course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Subspaces, Span, and Linear Independence
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid abstract language unless the problem needs it.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to test whether a set is a subspace, describe a span, decide whether a vector is in a span, and check linear independence.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start by writing: span = all linear combinations.
Say: a subspace is a set where linear combinations stay inside the set.
Say: linear independence means no vector is redundant.
Do Problem 1 to connect subspaces with homogeneous equations.
Do Problem 2 to practice span membership and independence in the same computation.
Do Problem 3 if time remains for parameter values that make vectors dependent.
sayThisFirst: Span means every possible linear combination of the given vectors.
A subspace must contain the zero vector and be closed under adding and scaling.
For this class, many subspaces come from homogeneous equations like Ax=0.
Linear independence means the only way to make zero is to use all zero coefficients.
Dependent means at least one vector is redundant because it can be made from the others.
boardPlan: Write the three words at the top: subspace, span, independence.
Under subspace, write: contains 0, closed under addition, closed under scaling.
Under span, write: all combinations c1v1 + c2v2 + ...
Under independence, write: c1v1 + c2v2 + ... = 0 only has the trivial solution.
Use Problem 1 to show a plane through the origin as a subspace.
Use Problem 2 to show how solving a system answers span membership.
Use Problem 3 to show that determinant zero means dependence for three vectors in R^3.
keyFormulas: \text{For } n \text{ vectors in } \mathbb{R}^n,\; \det A\neq 0 \Longleftrightarrow \text{the columns are independent.}
\text{A homogeneous solution set } Ax=0 \text{ is always a subspace.}
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check that a subspace contains the zero vector.
Saying a set is a subspace just because it looks like a line or plane. It must pass through the origin.
Confusing 'b is in the span' with 'the vectors are independent.' These are related but different questions.
Thinking that three vectors in R^3 are automatically independent. They are not; you still need to check.
Using row vectors and column vectors inconsistently when setting up the matrix.
notationWarnings: Write \operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2\}, not just span v1 v2.
Linear independence uses the equation c_1v_1+\cdots+c_kv_k=0. The right side is zero.
For span membership, the equation is c_1v_1+\cdots+c_kv_k=b. The right side is the target vector.
If vectors are columns of a matrix, keep them as columns when solving.

problems.title: Subspace from a homogeneous equation
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[ W=\{(x,y,z)\in\mathbb{R}^3: x-2y+z=0\}. \] Show that \(W\) is a subspace of \(\mathbb{R}^3\), and write \(W\) as the span of two vectors.
problems.whatToSayBefore: This is a good example because the equation equals zero. Homogeneous equations usually give subspaces. We still want to show what the set actually looks like.

visualSpec.purpose: Show that the equation x-2y+z=0 describes a plane through the origin spanned by two direction vectors.
boardSketchInstructions: Draw a tilted plane passing through the origin.
Draw two arrows in the plane from the origin: (2,1,0) and (-1,0,1).
Write W = span{(2,1,0), (-1,0,1)} beside the sketch.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The basis vectors both satisfy x-2y+z=0 and are not scalar multiples of each other, so they span the plane.

walkthrough.label: Check the zero vector
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The plane goes through the origin, which is a good sign for being a subspace.

walkthrough.label: Explain closure simply
walkthrough.latex: \text{If }u,v\in W,\text{ then }u\text{ and }v\text{ both satisfy }x-2y+z=0.\text{ Any linear combination also satisfies this homogeneous equation.}
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Because the equation is linear and equals zero, adding solutions or scaling a solution still gives zero.

walkthrough.label: Parametrize the equation
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Choose the variables that are easy to make free. Here y and z are convenient.

walkthrough.label: Write a general vector in W
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This line is the payoff: it shows every vector in W is built from two direction vectors.

walkthrough.label: State the span
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: A plane through the origin usually needs two independent direction vectors.

commonMistakes: Only checking that the zero vector is in W. That is necessary, but not the full subspace test.
Forgetting that the equation being equal to zero is what makes the closure argument easy.
Solving for x incorrectly: from x-2y+z=0, we get x=2y-z.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 3: Subspaces, Span, Linear Independence, Bases, Column Space, and Null Space

problems.title: Span membership and independence
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(v_1=(1,2,1)\), \(v_2=(0,1,-1)\), \(v_3=(2,5,0)\), and \(b=(3,8,-1)\). Is \(b\) in \(\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2,v_3\}\)? Also decide whether \(v_1,v_2,v_3\) are linearly independent.
problems.whatToSayBefore: This problem has two different questions. Span membership asks if we can make b. Independence asks if the vectors themselves have redundancy.

visualSpec.purpose: Show three vectors from the origin and connect determinant nonzero with spanning all of R^3.
boardSketchInstructions: Draw three arrows from the origin and label them v1, v2, and v3.
Do not try to draw the coordinates perfectly; the important point is that the determinant is nonzero.
Write the matrix with v1, v2, v3 as columns.
Show that det(A) = -1, so the three vectors are independent and span R^3.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: With the vectors as columns, det(A)=-1, so they are independent. The target vector satisfies b=-v1+2v3.

walkthrough.label: Set up the span equation
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: To ask whether b is in the span, we solve for the coefficients.

walkthrough.label: Put the vectors as columns
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The columns are the vectors we are combining. This is the cleanest setup.

walkthrough.label: Solve for the coefficients
walkthrough.latex: \begin{aligned} c_1+2c_3&=3,\\ 2c_1+c_2+5c_3&=8,\\ c_1-c_2&=-1. \end{aligned} \quad \text{This gives } c_1=-1,\; c_2=0,\; c_3=2.
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Once we find coefficients, b is in the span.

walkthrough.label: Write the span conclusion
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is the certificate that b is in the span.

walkthrough.label: Check independence
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: For three vectors in R^3, a nonzero determinant means independent. It also means they span all of R^3.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: b\in\operatorname{span}\{v_1,v_2,v_3\}\text{ because }b=-v_1+2v_3.\text{ Also, }v_1,v_2,v_3\text{ are linearly independent because }\det A=-1\neq 0.
commonMistakes: Putting v1, v2, v3 as rows instead of columns without thinking about what the coefficients multiply.
Stopping after showing b is in the span and forgetting to answer independence.
Thinking that c2=0 means the set is dependent. It does not. This coefficient solution was for b, not for zero.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 3: Span membership and linear independence

problems.title: A parameter value that makes vectors dependent
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(a_1=(1,0,2)\), \(a_2=(2,1,3)\), and \(a_3=(k,2,5)\). For what value of \(k\) are \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) linearly dependent? For that value of \(k\), give a nontrivial dependence relation.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Three vectors in R^3 are dependent exactly when the determinant of the matrix with those vectors as columns is zero.

visualSpec.purpose: Show that the determinant measures the signed volume of the parallelepiped formed by the three vectors; dependence happens when the volume becomes zero.
boardSketchInstructions: Draw a small parallelepiped from three vectors and label its volume det(A).
Say: if the volume is zero, the vectors collapse into a plane and are dependent.
Write A with a1, a2, a3 as columns.
Compute det(A)=7-2k and set it equal to zero.
At k=7/2, write the relation a1 - 4a2 + 2a3 = 0.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The determinant of A=[a1 a2 a3] is 7-2k, so dependence occurs at k=7/2. At that value, a1-4a2+2a3=0.

walkthrough.label: Build the matrix with the vectors as columns
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Always pause and make sure the vectors are columns.

walkthrough.label: Use determinant zero for dependence
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The determinant is the quick test because we have three vectors in R^3.

walkthrough.label: Set the determinant equal to zero
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Dependence happens when determinant equals zero, not when it is nonzero.

walkthrough.label: Find a dependence relation
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Now we want coefficients, not all zero, that make the zero vector.

walkthrough.label: Check the relation
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This proves dependence because the coefficients 1, -4, and 2 are not all zero.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: \boxed{k=\frac72}.\text{ For this value, one nontrivial relation is }\boxed{a_1-4a_2+2a_3=0}.
commonMistakes: Setting the determinant nonzero instead of zero for dependence.
Forgetting that the dependence relation must equal the zero vector.
Giving k=7/2 but not giving the requested nontrivial relation.
problems.practiceLabPointer: Practice Lab Section 3: Linear independence and parameter dependence

wrapUpQuestions: What is the difference between asking if b is in the span and asking if the vectors are independent?
Why do homogeneous equations often produce subspaces?
What does determinant zero mean geometrically for three vectors in R^3?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 3
linksToWebsite.reason: More practice with subspaces, span, linear independence, bases, column space, and null space.

qualityControlNotes: Checked that W is a plane through the origin and that the displayed spanning vectors satisfy x-2y+z=0.
Checked Problem 2: det([v1 v2 v3])=-1 and b=-v1+2v3.
Checked Problem 3: det(A)=7-2k, so k=7/2; at that value a1-4a2+2a3=0.
Notation uses operatorname{span} and distinguishes span membership equations from independence equations.
reviewSummary: Session 05 emphasizes simple whiteboard language and uses exact visualSpec data for the plane, vector span, and determinant-volume interpretations. Arithmetic and LaTeX were checked before export.
